title.english: Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream: Traditional Fishing Practices
title.teReo: Ngā Kaitiaki o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Ngā Tikanga Hī Ika Tawhito

subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore traditional Ngāti Kahungunu fishing practices specific to the Mangakōtukutuku stream, learning about the pūrākau (legend) of the stream's namesake - the fantail who guided ancestors to abundant fishing grounds. The lesson connects to local environmental restoration efforts and the college's role as kaitiaki of this significant waterway.
learningObjectives: Identify and use te reo Māori vocabulary for local native fish species (inanga, kōura, pātiki) and traditional fishing methods (hīnaki, pā kahawai)
Explain the significance of the Mangakōtukutuku stream in Ngāti Kahungunu traditions using appropriate cultural protocols
Create and present a simple mihi that acknowledges the stream as an ancestor and expresses kaitiakitanga responsibilities
Design a bilingual information panel (English/Te Reo) about traditional fishing practices for the college's stream restoration area

activities.title: He Whakawhiti Kōrero - Sharing Stories of the Awa
activities.description: Students listen to local kaumātua share pūrākau of Mangakōtukutuku stream and learn basic mihi that acknowledge the awa as tupuna. Practice introducing themselves and their connection to the waterway.

activities.title: Ngā Kupu o Te Awa - Language of the Waterway
activities.description: Hands-on vocabulary building using flash cards and real artifacts (hīnaki, fishing tools). Students learn names of local fish, plants, and fishing methods through games and matching activities at the stream's edge.

activities.title: Te Hī Ika Tawhito - Traditional Fishing Demonstration
activities.description: Local expert demonstrates traditional hīnaki (eel trap) making and placement. Students practice giving and following instructions in te reo for setting traps and identifying appropriate fishing locations.

activities.title: He Panui Kaitiaki - Guardian Reading
activities.description: Students analyze bilingual texts about local conservation efforts and identify key te reo phrases expressing kaitiakitanga. Create simple sentences about protecting the stream using sentence starters.

activities.title: Te Mahi Toi - Creative Expression
activities.description: Students work in pairs to design and draft bilingual information panels about traditional fishing practices, incorporating learned vocabulary and cultural concepts for display along the stream walkway.

resources: Local kaumātua Hone Smith (Ngāti Kahungunu fishing knowledge holder)
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Traditional Māori Fishing'
Hawke's Bay Regional Council stream monitoring kits
'He Pātaka Kupu' Māori language dictionary for technical terms

tasks: Oral presentation of mihi acknowledging the Mangakōtukutuku stream
Vocabulary quiz on fish species and fishing terms
Completed draft of bilingual information panel with minimum 5 te reo sentences

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic te reo Māori patterns and vocabulary to communicate about immediate environment
Social Sciences: Identity, Culture, and Organization - Level 4: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs and traditions
Science: Living World - Level 4: Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
